title: From start-dev to Production
description:
## A Risky Production Setup

Someone deployed Keycloak to production by running the container with **`start-dev`** and the default **H2** database. It works in the demo but is neither safe nor scalable.

What is the correct production configuration?

options.text: Run **`start`** (production mode) with a configured **hostname**, an **external production database** like **PostgreSQL** (not H2), and **TLS** — and run it clustered/HA as needed
Keep using **`start-dev`** but add more memory so H2 can scale
Stay on **H2** but run several instances pointed at separate H2 files for redundancy
Disable TLS to reduce CPU load and speed up logins in production

explanation: `start-dev` exists **only for development**: it enables dev conveniences and defaults to the in-memory/file **H2** database, which is not for production. Production uses **`start`** with: an explicitly configured **hostname**, an **external, production-grade database** such as **PostgreSQL** (also MySQL/MariaDB/Oracle/MSSQL), and **TLS**; instances can be run **clustered (HA)** behind a load balancer sharing the same DB. (a) is correct. (b) and (c) cling to H2 — which can't be the shared, durable store a clustered deployment needs (separate H2 files means inconsistent, un-shared state). (d) disabling TLS exposes credentials and tokens and is the opposite of hardening.

hints.title: dev vs prod entrypoint
hints.body: The container image is quay.io/keycloak/keycloak. `start-dev` = development; `start` = production and will refuse to boot until required settings (db, hostname, TLS/proxy) are configured.
